model_narrative: We want to verify whether a particular target chemical can be synthesized from the chemicals we have on hand using a fixed library of reactions. Some chemicals are available from the start as raw materials or catalysts, and a few others are known to be unavailable. Each reaction produces one chemical by consuming a group of other chemicals. For every chemical we decide whether it can be present in the system. The objective is to make the presence indicator of the target chemical as small as possible, which reveals whether that target can be produced at all.

sets.name: R
sets.doc: the chemical reactions in the library; each reaction produces one chemical by consuming a group of other chemicals

sets.name: C
sets.doc: the chemicals in the system, numbered from 1 to 34, covering raw materials, catalysts, intermediates, and the target chemical; chemical 6 is the synthesis target

sets.name: RP
sets.doc: the valid reaction and product pairs; each pair names a reaction together with the single chemical that this reaction produces, and for that pair the reaction has a known group of input chemicals it consumes

params.name: rm
params.doc: a zero or one flag indexed by reaction, product chemical, and reactant chemical; it is one when the third chemical is one of the inputs the reaction consumes to make the second chemical, and zero otherwise; only the entries that are one are present, all others default to zero

vars.name: y
vars.doc: one if the chemical can be present or produced in the system, zero if it is absent

constraints.name: rm
constraints.intent: For each reaction paired with the chemical it produces, that chemical can be present only when the reaction has its inputs in hand. Require that whenever the produced chemical is present, the reaction must be able to draw on its required input chemicals being present as well, so that the produced chemical cannot be present unless the inputs this reaction consumes to make it are present. Reactions that do not produce a given chemical place no requirement for that pairing.

constraints.name: y[c]
constraints.intent: Pin down the chemicals whose availability is already settled. Every chemical that is available on hand from the start must be present in the system, and every chemical that is known to be unavailable must be absent. Chemicals whose availability is not predetermined are left free to be decided by the rest of the model.

query: Our magnesium turnings, chemical 22, come in on the same consolidated order as the chromium trioxide we already can't source — if chromium trioxide, chemical 16, stays off the books, the magnesium ships with it. Fold that linkage in and tell me whether acetone still comes out producible.

query: Maintenance wants to pull the halogen storage bay offline for requalification, which would take both iodine, chemical 26, and chlorine, chemical 33, out of the system for the duration. Run that window — where does acetone producibility land?
expected_pyomo:
model.material_cons[26].deactivate()
model.material_cons[33].deactivate()
def _halogen_bay(model, c):
    return model.y[c] <= 0 if c in [26, 33] else Constraint.Skip
model.halogen_bay_down = Constraint(model.C, rule=_halogen_bay)

query: Procurement wants to consolidate the front-end feed: at most one of chemicals 1, 2, and 3 in the mix. Under that restriction alone, can we still get to acetone?
expected_pyomo:
model.material_cons[2].deactivate()
model.material_cons[3].deactivate()
model.amo_123 = Constraint(expr=model.y[1] + model.y[2] + model.y[3] <= 1)

query: We're looking at a sourcing scenario where we can keep at most one of chemicals 11, 12, and 13 on hand. What does that do to whether acetone can be produced?
expected_pyomo:
model.material_cons[12].deactivate()
model.material_cons[13].deactivate()
model.amo_111213 = Constraint(expr=model.y[11] + model.y[12] + model.y[13] <= 1)

query: The warehouse team is pitching a stocking rule for diethyl ether, chemical 10: keep it on hand only if at least one of its downstream partners — isobutylene, chemical 18, or formic acid, chemical 20 — is present in the system. What does the run show for acetone under that rule?

query: Safety keeps flagging that sodium cyanide, chemical 31, sits on the floor even though the ozone feed, chemical 19, is unavailable and the chromia guard bed, chemical 29, never shows up in the plan. Shouldn't cyanide be allowed on site only when both of those are present in the system? What's the model seeing that I'm not?
expected_pyomo:
model.material_cons[31].deactivate()
model.nacn_safety_gate = Constraint(expr=2 * model.y[31] <= model.y[19] + model.y[29])

query: Chlorine, chemical 33, rides the same gas contract as ozone, chemical 19, and ozone has been off the books for months — yet the plan leans on chlorine like it's guaranteed. Shouldn't chlorine track ozone's availability one-for-one? What's keeping the model from living with that?

query: We never charge the chromia catalyst bed — chemical 29 isn't in the plan anywhere — but the route still draws on methane, chemical 25, and oxygen, chemical 28, as if those runs were free-standing. Shouldn't each of those feeds be allowed only when the chromia bed is present? Walk me through what the model's seeing if we hold it to that.
expected_pyomo:
model.material_cons[25].deactivate()
model.material_cons[28].deactivate()
def _chromia_gate(model, c):
    return model.y[c] <= model.y[29] if c in [25, 28] else Constraint.Skip
model.chromia_gate = Constraint(model.C, rule=_chromia_gate)

query: Our wet bench supplies both the process water, chemical 12, and the sulfuric acid, chemical 17, and it's only commissioned while the formic-acid polishing step — chemical 20 — is active in the system. Formic acid never appears in the plan, so shouldn't both of those feeds be shut off with it? What's the tradeoff the model is hiding?
expected_pyomo:
model.material_cons[12].deactivate()
model.material_cons[17].deactivate()
model.wet_bench_gate = Constraint(expr=model.y[12] + model.y[17] <= 2 * model.y[20])

query: Iodine, chemical 26, and sulfuric acid, chemical 17, share a single certified storage slot, so exactly one of the two is on hand at any given time — and we don't get to choose which. The plan acts like both are guaranteed. What's the model seeing for acetone once we hold it to one at a time?
